text: The surgeon begins the procedure by incising the peritoneum along the root of the small bowel mesentery, opening the posterior planes. The second part of the duodenum is visualized and mobilized from the overlying right colon mesentery. Subsequently, the right colon is mobilized in a medial to lateral fashion.

The surgeon begins the procedure by incising the peritoneum along the root of the small bowel mesentery, opening up the posterior planes. The second part of the duodenum is visualized and mobilized from the overlying right colon mesentery. The right colon is then mobilized in a medial to lateral fashion.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreatic head and the C of the duodenum from the overlying right colon mesentery. The alecolic pedicle is elevated, revealing the posterior plane. Dissection continues towards the root of the alecolic pedicle, which is exposed. The transverse mesocolon is elevated, and the peritoneum is cut towards its root. The right colic vein is isolated, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreatic head and the duodenum from the overlying right colon mesentery. The ileocolic pedicle is elevated in the peritoneum, opening up the posterior plane to meet the posterior dissection. Dissection continues towards the root of the ileocolic pedicle, which is exposed. The transverse mesocolon is elevated, and the peritoneum is incised towards its root. The right colic vein is isolated, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon dissects the middle colic pedicle, revealing the middle colic artery and vein. The anterior branch of the middle colic artery is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by the middle colic vein and posterior branch, completing the division of the middle colic pedicle. Dissection continues towards the root of the ileocolic pedicle, where the right colic artery is clipped and divided, followed by the ileocolic pedicle itself.

The surgeon dissects the middle colic pedicle, revealing the middle colic artery and vein. The anterior branch of the artery is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by the middle colic vein and posterior branch, which are also clipped and divided to complete the division of the pedicle.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the root of the ileocolic pedicle, encountering the right colic artery originating at this location. The artery is clipped and divided, followed by the clipping and division of the ileocolic pedicle itself.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to take down the lateral attachments of the right colon, progressing towards the hepatic flexure. The omentum is separated from the transverse colon to access the lesser sac, moving towards the left. The right gastroepiploic pedicle is elevated, and dissection continues along this pedicle to connect with the posterior dissection, revealing the ligated medial colic pedicles. The free end of the transverse mesocolon is divided, and the remaining attachments of the right colon are severed by sharp dissection, completing the mobilization of the right colon.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to take down the lateral attachments of the right colon, progressing towards the hepatic flexure. The omentum is separated from the transverse colon to access the lesser sac, moving leftward. The right gastroepiploic pedicle is elevated, and dissection continues along this pedicle to connect with the posterior dissection, revealing the ligated medial colic pedicles. The free end of the transverse mesocolon is divided, extending leftward, and the remaining attachments of the right colon are sharply dissected to complete its mobilization.

The surgeon begins rectal mobilization by incising the right leaf of the rectal nascent tree, opening the posterior planes and proceeding medial to lateral. Dissection continues towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), which is exposed down to its origin. The left colic artery is inadvertently injured, controlled with bipolar forceps, and isolated with a clip to achieve hemostasis. Nodal dissection is completed down to the root of the IMA, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon begins rectal mobilization by incising the right leaf of the rectal nascent tree, opening up the posterior planes and proceeding medial to lateral. Dissection continues towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is dissected down to its origin. The left colic artery is inadvertently injured and controlled with bipolar forceps. The origin of the left colic artery is isolated with a clip, and bipolar hemostasis is achieved. Nodal dissection is completed down to the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is clipped and divided.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon in a medial to lateral direction within the avascular posterior plane, carefully avoiding the left gonadal vessels and left ureter. The dissection proceeds superiorly, posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein, reaching the lower border of the pancreas, and laterally extending to the left kidney up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon in a medial to lateral direction within the avascular posterior plane, carefully avoiding the left gonadal vessels and left ureter. The dissection proceeds superiorly, posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein, reaching the lower border of the pancreas, and laterally extending to the left kidney up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum at the duodenal jejunal flexure and elevates the free edge of the transverse mesocolon to visualize the lesser sac. The transverse mesocolon is divided from the lower border of the pancreas. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated at its origin, clipped, and divided. An accessory artery is encountered, clipped, and divided. The remaining attachments of the transverse mesocolon to the lower border of the pancreas are taken down, proceeding towards the splenic flexure.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum at the duodenal jejunal flexure and elevates the free edge of the transverse mesocolon to visualize the lesser sac. The transverse mesocolon is divided from the lower border of the pancreas, isolating the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin, which is clipped and divided. An accessory artery is encountered, clipped, and divided. The remaining attachments of the transverse mesocolon to the lower border of the pancreas are taken down, proceeding towards the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to take down the lateral attachments of the left colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure. The attachments at the splenic flexure are dissected, and the gauze piece is encountered, indicating communication with the medial dissection, leading to complete mobilization of the abdominal colon.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to take down the lateral attachments of the left colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure. The attachments at the splenic flexure are dissected, and the gauze piece is encountered, indicating communication with the medial dissection, leading to complete mobilization of the abdominal colon.

Rectal mobilization begins at the sacral promontory, with the surgeon opening the posterior planes. The hypogastric nerves are visible, and the left nerve is divided first, followed by the right, to open the presacral plane. The presacral plane is developed under the pelvic floor in the midline, proceeding around the rectum on the left side, then the right. The right ureter is identified at the pelvic brim, medialized towards the urethral recycle junction, and circumferentially mobilized to meet the posterior presacral plane up to the pelvic brim.

The surgeon begins rectal mobilization at the sacral promontory, opening the posterior planes. The hypogastric nerves are visible, with the left nerve divided first, followed by the right, to open the presacral plane. The presacral plane is developed under the pelvic floor in the midline, proceeding around the rectum on the left side, then the right. The right ureter is identified at the pelvic brim and medialized along its length towards the urethral vesical junction, circumferentially mobilized to meet the posterior presacral plane at the pelvic brim.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the obliterated umbilical artery on the right side. The right vas deferens is clipped and divided. The lateral paravesical space is opened, exposing the obliterated umbilical artery to its origin, which is then isolated, clipped, and divided. Subsequently, the superior vesical artery is clipped and divided.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the obliterated umbilical artery on the right side. The right vas deferens is clipped and divided. The lateral paravesical space is opened, exposing the obliterated umbilical artery to its origin, which is then isolated, clipped, and divided. The superior vesical artery is also clipped and divided. The posterior presacral plane is developed further towards the right lateral pelvic sidewall. The lateral paravesical space is expanded until the obturator internus muscle is visualized. The bladder pedicles, located between the lateral paravesical space and the posterior presacral space, are identified. The inferior vesical pedicles are clipped and divided, and dissection continues until the levator muscle, where the endopelvic fascia on the right is incised.

The surgeon further develops the posterior presacral plane towards the right lateral pelvic sidewall, visualizing the obturator internus muscle. The bladder pedicles are located between the lateral paravesical space and the posterior presacral space. The inferior vesicle pedicles are clipped and divided, and dissection continues until the levator muscle is reached to incise the endopelvic fascia on the right.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, continuing towards the left lateral pelvic sidewall. The obliterated umbilical artery on the left is isolated, clipped, and divided. The lateral paravesical space is developed until the obturator internus muscle is visible. Bladder pedicles between these spaces are serially clipped and divided down to the pelvic floor. The retropubic avascular space is opened, revealing the puboprostatic ligaments on both sides. The endopelvic fascia is incised to separate the puborectalis muscle from the lateral aspect of the prostate.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, continuing posteriorly towards the left lateral pelvic sidewall. The obliterated umbilical artery on the left is isolated, clipped, and divided. The lateral paravesical space is developed until the obturator internus muscle is visualized. Bladder pedicles between these spaces are serially clipped and divided down to the pelvic floor. The retropubic avascular space is opened, revealing the puboprostatic ligaments on both sides. The endopelvic fascia is incised to separate the puborectalis muscle from the lateral aspect of the prostate.

The surgeon incises the puboprostatic ligaments to isolate the dorsal venous complex. The right and left ureters are clipped at the ureterovesical junction and divided. A figure-of-eight suture with number one vicryl is placed on the dorsal venous complex for hemostasis. The dorsal venous complex is coagulated and divided using bipolar cautery until reaching the urethra. The anterior wall of the urethra is incised, exposing the Foley catheter, completing the abdominal part of the procedure.

The surgeon incises the puboprostatic ligaments to isolate the dorsal venous complex. The right and left ureters are clipped at the ureterovesical junction and divided. A figure-of-eight suture with number one vicryl is placed on the dorsal venous complex for hemostasis. The dorsal venous complex is coagulated and divided using bipolar cautery until reaching the urethra. The anterior wall of the urethra is incised, exposing the Foley's catheter, completing the abdominal part of the procedure.

The surgeon completes the perineal dissection and exteriorizes the specimen through a lower abdomen mini laparotomy. An ileal conduit is formed, and the ureteric anastomosis is performed. The ileostomy is matured through the existing sigmoid stoma site.
